title: US charges Cuba's Raúl Castro with murder over 1996 downing of two planes
text: The US has charged former Cuban leader Raúl Castro with conspiracy to kill US nationals and other crimes over the 1996 downing of two planes between Cuba and Florida. The case unveiled on Wednesday accuses Castro and five others in the shooting down of the aircraft belonging to Cuban-American group Brothers to the Rescue and killing four people, including three Americans. Castro, now 94, was then head of the country's armed forces and faced international condemnation over the crash. As the US seeks to exert increasing pressure on Cuba's communist rule, President Miguel Díaz-Canel called the charges a political manoeuvre, devoid of any legal foundation. Speaking at Freedom Tower in Miami, Acting Attorney General Todd Blanche announced that the US would also charge Castro with destruction of aircraft, and four individual counts of murder. The murder charges each carry a maximum penalty of death or life imprisonment. The US has issued sanctions on the country and imposed a blockade on oil to Cuba that has resulted in blackouts and food shortages. Nearly 95 years old, Castro, the brother of late Cuban leader Fidel Castro, remains an influential figure acknowledged on the island as the surviving leader of the Cuban Revolution.

title: Watch: Why is the US going after Cuba's Raúl Castro 30 years on?
text: BBC correspondent Will Grant reports from Havana, hours after the US charged former Cuban leader Raúl Castro with conspiracy to kill US nationals and other crimes. The case unveiled on Wednesday accuses Castro and five others over the 1996 downing of two planes belonging to Cuban-American group Brothers to the Rescue. Four people were killed, including three Americans. Cuban President Miguel Díaz-Canel has called the charges a political manoeuvre, devoid of any legal foundation. Nearly 95 years old, Castro, the brother of late Cuban leader Fidel Castro, remains an influential figure, acknowledged on the island as the surviving leader of the Cuban Revolution.

title: SpaceX files for stock market debut that could make Elon Musk a trillionaire
text: Elon Musk's SpaceX has revealed its plans to go public in the US, allowing people to trade shares in the firm on the stock market. SpaceX makes rockets, offers a satellite internet service called Starlink, and also owns Musk's controversial artificial intelligence firm xAI. The initial public offering on the US stock market is set to be the largest in Wall Street history and could start next month under the ticker symbol SPCX. SpaceX values itself at 1.25 trillion dollars, and Musk's majority ownership of the company means his share could be worth more than 600 billion dollars. Last year, Space Exploration Technologies brought in 18.6 billion dollars in revenue but had a net loss of 4.9 billion dollars. Meanwhile, its balance sheet shows it has 102 billion dollars in assets but also carries 60.5 billion dollars of debt. The IPO filing comes just days after Musk lost a high-profile legal battle against rival AI company OpenAI and its boss, Sam Altman.

title: Retired police officer jailed over Charlie Kirk post settles lawsuit for more than $800K
text: A Tennessee man who was jailed for more than a month following his arrest over a Facebook post related to the killing of Charlie Kirk has settled an unlawful incarceration lawsuit for 835,000 dollars. Larry Bushart, a retired police officer, spent 37 days behind bars before authorities dropped the felony charges against him, during which he lost his post-retirement job and missed the birth of his grandchild. He was arrested by the Perry County Sheriff's Office for sharing a meme in a thread about a vigil honouring the conservative activist. Bushart remained behind bars because he was unable to pay a 2 million dollar bond. His lawsuit alleged violations of his First and Fourth Amendment rights.

title: Far-right Israeli minister condemned for taunting handcuffed Gaza flotilla activists
text: There has been international condemnation of Israel's treatment of pro-Palestinian activists who were on board a Gaza-bound aid flotilla intercepted by Israeli naval forces. The US, the UK, France, Italy and Canada expressed outrage after far-right National Security Minister Itamar Ben-Gvir posted a video showing himself taunting activists kneeling with their hands tied behind their backs. His actions drew rare criticism from Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu, who said they were not in line with Israel's values. More than 50 boats taking part in the Global Sumud Flotilla set sail from Turkey carrying aid. Four hundred and thirty people from more than 40 countries were on board. The Gaza war was triggered by the Hamas-led attack on southern Israel on 7 October 2023, when about 1,200 people were killed and 251 others were taken hostage.

title: Trump says he will speak to Taiwan's president in break from protocol
text: US President Donald Trump says he will talk to Taiwanese leader Lai Ching-te about a possible arms sale, in what would be a sharp departure from diplomatic tradition. US and Taiwanese leaders have not spoken directly since 1979 when Washington severed formal ties with Taiwan to recognise the Beijing government. Beijing claims Taiwan as its territory and has not ruled out taking it by force. Trump has said he has yet to decide if the sale of a 14 billion dollar arms package to Taiwan will go ahead. During Trump's visit to Beijing, China had made it clear that Taiwan was one of the biggest issues in its relationship with the US, with Xi warning of conflict between the two superpowers if handled poorly.

title: How Trump's IRS settlement could block tax audits of him, his family and their businesses
text: The US Department of Justice has announced that this week's unprecedented settlement of President Donald Trump's lawsuit over the leaking of his tax returns blocks the IRS from reviewing tax filings that Trump, his family and his businesses made in the past. In January, Trump and his two eldest sons sued the IRS for 10 billion dollars over leaks of their business and personal tax returns. It was the first time a president had sued the US government. The one-page addendum states that the United States is forever barred from conducting examinations or reviews related to taxes filed by Trump, his family members and their companies. The justice department agreed to establish a 1.776 billion dollar Anti-Weaponization Fund.

title: DR Congo cancels World Cup training camp over Ebola outbreak
text: The Democratic Republic of Congo has cancelled its pre-World Cup training camp in the capital Kinshasa because of an Ebola outbreak in the east of the country. Preparations have been moved to Belgium amid the upsurge, which is thought to have killed more than 130 people. The World Health Organization has called the outbreak a public health emergency of international concern, but said it was not at pandemic level. DR Congo are due to play friendly matches against Denmark on 3 June in Belgium and Chile on 9 June in Spain ahead of the World Cup finals. They play their first match of the tournament in Houston on 17 June against Portugal. The outbreak has been caused by a rare species of Ebola known as Bundibugyo and there is currently no vaccine for this species.

title: From AI to interceptors, Ukraine is trying to drone-proof its skies
text: Kyiv held a funeral for two sisters this week. 12-year-old Liubava and her 17-year-old sister Vira were among 24 civilians killed by a Russian missile which reduced their residential block to rubble. This is the human cost of the largest sustained Russian aerial assault so far, with 1,500 drones and 56 missiles fired at Ukraine within 48 hours. According to President Volodymyr Zelensky, 94 percent of those long-range drones and 73 percent of the missiles were successfully intercepted. Ukraine is now producing more than 1,000 interceptor drones a day. These P1-SUN interceptors are 3D-printed and cost around 1,000 dollars each, far less than the 50,000 dollar Shahed attack drones they are designed to destroy.

title: Nvidia's record result fails to impress investors
text: Chip giant Nvidia reported yet another record quarter with sales and profits beating expectations, suggesting there is no slowdown in sight for the artificial intelligence boom. Nvidia is a central player in AI infrastructure, providing chips to leading AI model developers including OpenAI and Meta. The company reported first-quarter revenue was up 85 percent on the year to 81.6 billion dollars, while net income more than tripled to 58.3 billion dollars. However, shares in the company fell 1.6 percent in after-hours trading. Nvidia is the world's most valuable company with a stock market value of around 5.3 trillion dollars. Chief executive Jensen Huang told analysts that demand has gone parabolic and that the era of agentic AI is here.
